title: Low-battery radio listening schedule
summary: A battery-saving information schedule that prevents constant scanning, rumor panic, and missed official updates.
content: When power is limited, radio and phones are lifelines and battery drains. Set listening windows, choose official stations or known local sources, write only confirmed instructions, and keep rumor notes separate. At least two people should know how to operate the device.
steps: Record source, time, instruction, and confirmation status.
Turn off between windows unless active alerts are expected.
warnings: Do not spread claims from unknown sources as fact.
Do not drain the last battery on entertainment during crisis.
common_mistakes: Scanning for hours with no log.
Writing summaries without source or time.
alternatives: Run a smaller version of Low-battery radio listening schedule with one responsible person, one helper, and one visible paper note.
Use a safer fallback location or method for Low-battery radio listening schedule if the first choice becomes wet, crowded, contaminated, dark, unstable, or unsafe.
Pause Low-battery radio listening schedule and verify the written record with a second person before moving scarce supplies.
failure_signs: People cannot explain who is responsible for Low-battery radio listening schedule or when the next check happens.
Supplies connected to Low-battery radio listening schedule are unlabelled, damp, contaminated, damaged, missing, or being used for the wrong purpose.

The routine ignores fire risk, carbon monoxide risk, tool condition, batteries, and safe isolation and starts creating new risk instead of reducing existing risk during “Low-battery radio listening schedule”
when_not_to_use: Do not use Low-battery radio listening schedule as a reason to delay evacuation, emergency calls, medical care, fire response, or official local instructions.
Do not use Low-battery radio listening schedule when the location is structurally unsafe, chemically suspicious, smoky, flooded, violent, or otherwise immediately dangerous.
Do not improvise hazardous repairs, dosing, chemical handling, weapon use, or electrical/gas work under the label of Low-battery radio listening schedule.
short_term: For the first hours of Low-battery radio listening schedule, keep the action small, visible, and reversible: write the goal, assign one owner, protect vulnerable people, separate safe from unsafe supplies, and set a review time.
long_term: If Low-battery radio listening schedule remains necessary for days or weeks, rotate roles, preserve written lessons, replace fragile supplies, and train another person so the group does not depend on one expert.
if_method_fails: If Low-battery radio listening schedule fails, stop escalation, move to the safest fallback, record what failed, protect people first, and restart only with a simpler written plan.
environment_notes: Adapt Low-battery radio listening schedule to the real environment: check fire risk, carbon monoxide risk, tool condition, batteries, and safe isolation, local weather, building condition, water access, light, noise, animals, neighbours, and safe exits before repeating the routine.
